coredata.dc:title: Amino-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and silver-graphene based nanocomposites: Ultrafast charge transfer and a proof-of-concept study for bioimaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Karatay, Ahmet
Erdener, Diğdem
Gürcan, Cansu
Yildiz, Elif Akhuseyin
Yilmazer, Açelya
Boyacıoğlu, Bahadır
Unver, Hüseyin
Yıldız, Mustafa
Elmali, Ayhan
coredata.dc:description: Recent advances in nanotechnology have led to the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for functional imaging and cancer therapy. Thanks to the physicochemical properties of graphene, GQDs have been functionalized and modified to improve their biological properties. In this study, polyethyleneimine functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PEI N-doped GQDs) and silver nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs) nanocomposites have been developed. The PEI N-doped GQDs show a strong emission spectrum at maximum 446 nm with 356 nm excitation wavelength while AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites indicate weak fluorescence intensity at maximum 446 nm with 344 nm excitation wavelength. Based on the femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy measurements, this emission quenching can be attributed that the excited state lifetime of PEI N-doped GQDs decreasing in the presence of AgNPs due to ultrafast electron transfer from PEI N-doped GQDs to AgNPs. It is also supported by theoretical results sensing ability of AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite is better than that of PEI N-doped GQDs. When lung adenocarcinoma cells (A549) were treated with GQDs, material-specific fluorescent signals were obtained from the cytoplasm of cells only treated with Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs, as compared with N-doped GQDs. Overall, this study shows that Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs are promising nanosystems for bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino functionalized N-doped GQDs
Ag nanocomposites
Ultrafast spectroscopy
Bioimaging
DFT method

Characterization of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs
In vitro imaging of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs
Characterization of the synthesized PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs (FT-IR, TEM, EDX)
UV–Vis, FMO, MEP surfaces and DOS analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM Analysis of a) PEI N-doped GQDs and b) AgNps PEI N-doped GQDs.
Steady state absorption and emission spectra of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNps PEI N-doped GQDs in H2O.

Femtosecond transient absorption spectra of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs PEI N-doped GQDs with different time delays. (
λ
ex
= 350 nm).

Time evolution of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs PEI N-doped GQDs at 446 nm probe wavelength (
λ
ex
= 350 nm).
DOS spectra with HOMO and LUMO visualization and MEP surfaces of the PEI N-doped GQDs (a) and AgNPs/ PEI and PEI N-doped GQDs (b) nanocomposites.

In vitro imaging of Ag/ PEI N-doped GQDs or PEI N-doped GQDs. A549 cells were treated with materials for 4 h. A) Cells were imaged under a fluorescent microscope (40X). Representative images were taken. DAPI was used to counterstain nuclei. B) Percentage of cell viability was assessed by LDH assay at 24 h.
Synthesis of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/ PEI and PEI N-doped GQDs.
The theoretical model of PEI N-doped GQDs (a) and AgNPs/ PEI and PEI N-doped GQDs (b).
Frontier Molecular Orbitals and UV–Visible analysis.

head.ce:title: Amino-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and silver-graphene based nanocomposites: Ultrafast charge transfer and a proof-of-concept study for bioimaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent advances in nanotechnology have led to the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for functional imaging and cancer therapy. Thanks to the physicochemical properties of graphene, GQDs have been functionalized and modified to improve their biological properties. In this study, polyethyleneimine functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PEI N-doped GQDs) and silver nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs) nanocomposites have been developed. The PEI N-doped GQDs show a strong emission spectrum at maximum 446 nm with 356 nm excitation wavelength while AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites indicate weak fluorescence intensity at maximum 446 nm with 344 nm excitation wavelength. Based on the femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy measurements, this emission quenching can be attributed that the excited state lifetime of PEI N-doped GQDs decreasing in the presence of AgNPs due to ultrafast electron transfer from PEI N-doped GQDs to AgNPs. It is also supported by theoretical results sensing ability of AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite is better than that of PEI N-doped GQDs. When lung adenocarcinoma cells (A549) were treated with GQDs, material-specific fluorescent signals were obtained from the cytoplasm of cells only treated with Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs, as compared with N-doped GQDs. Overall, this study shows that Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs are promising nanosystems for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging techniques are fundamental tools in clinical diagnosis and therapy assessment allowing noninvasive observation of pathological and physiological events. Fluorescent-based bioimaging approaches have been widely studied and until now various fluorescence agent probes have been developed [1]. Organic dyes which are widely used in bioimaging applications suffer several limitations including photobleaching, poor solubility, and weak stability [1,2]. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have been explored as an alternative to organic dyes however mainly their toxicity problems due to the presence of heavy metals limit their use in the clinic [3,4]. Following the discovery of graphene, graphene QDs have received much interest due to their interesting optical properties, GQDs emission properties, good photostability, chemical inertness, photo-electrochemical properties, and photocatalytic properties [5–14]. Since GQDs possess nano-sized single atomic layer, strong quantum confinement, and side effects in size between 1 and 10 nm, they have many interesting wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties. Due to their great scientific and technological interest, effective strategies have been developed to prepare GQDs [15,16].

Thanks to the surface properties of graphene, differently functionalized and modified materials can be obtained according to the desired application. For example, among different GQDs, N-doped GQDs (N-doped GQDs) have been prepared using a series of N-containing bases such as urea, and detailed structural and proprietary studies have shown the mechanism of formation of N-doped GQDs for adjustable optical emissions [17]. These materials have been demonstrated to have different bio applications including bioimaging [18–25]. By further modifying these materials, GQDs can gain new properties. For example, amino-functionalized GQDs have been prepared under mild conditions from graphene oxide (GO) via oxidative fragmentation [26–30]. In addition, nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with PEI (GQD-PEIs) have been synthesized and used for cancer theranostics and nucleic acid delivery [31–34]. Furthermore, graphene has been employed as excellent support for metal nanomaterials because of its unique structural and physicochemical properties. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with exceptional properties have received considerable attention in various fields; however, particle aggregation limits its application.Therefore, various researches are carried out to improve the synthesis, combination, properties, and applications of AgNPs and graphene-based nanocomposites [35–37]. Metal nanoparticles can further improve fluorescence imaging as they scatter the absorbed light very efficiently. In the case of organic molecular fluorophores, a low detection limit is difficult to achieve due to low quantum yield and low labeling ratio of dyes to targeted molecules. This limitation has been overcome by using fluorescent metal nanoparticles [38,39]. Various nanoparticles have been developed as remarkable imaging probes compared to conventional imaging fluorophores[40]. Nanomaterials are found to be advantageous over conventional imaging probes owing to their ability to provide in-depth information. Their high photostability aids in the visualization of the image for a longer period. Their ability to penetrate deeper at the tissue level and be taken up efficiently by cells make them promising bioimaging agents[41]. Furthermore, their highly loadable surface can allow extra features to be added to these nanoprobes. Due to all these properties, graphene-based nanomaterials are more suitable than other probes in the field of bioimaging. Based on this proof-of-concept study, AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites can be considered as potential materials for both imaging and diagnosis. Considering that various graphene-based materials and other 2D materials have been studied extensively for theranostic applications [42,43], it would be interesting to test these materials for such a purpose. For this purpose, experimental animal models should be implemented to fully characterize the biomedical capabilities of these materials. Future studies should also focus on their in vitro and in vivo toxicity and biocompatibility profiles.

In this work, amino-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Amino N-doped GQDs) were successfully prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as the carbon, nitrogen and amino group precursors, respectively. A simple method was used to prepare silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) based on the use of polyethyleneimine functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PEI N-doped GQDs) as a reducing agent and stabilizer. An ecofriendly and zero-cost approach has been used for the hydrothermal synthesis of more stable AgNPs nanocomposites using an aqueous extract of N-doped GQDs as a reducing and stabilizing agent to reduce silver nitrate. Recently, there have been several reports demonstrating that the introduced functional groups can modify the optical properties of GQDs and carbon dots [44–46]. The main aim of this work is to investigate the effect of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites on fluorescence behaviour and bioimaging. The PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites have been characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The observed emission quenching was investigated by the ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy experiments in the visible region. Later, a theoretical study was performed based on density functional theory (DFT) with Coulomb-attenuating method-Becke’s three parameters hybrid exchange–correlation functional (CAM-B3LYP) basis set for the UV–Vis analysis of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites. Finally, a proof-of-study was performed in vitro to explore the potential of these nanomaterials for bioimaging applications.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. The citric acid (CA), AgNO3, Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Mw: 1300, 50 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PEI functionalized N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: For the first time, polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized N-doped graphene quantum dots (PEI N-doped GQDs) containing N atoms have been synthesized using the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and PEI. Then, green synthesis of AgNP/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites was performed by reducing AgNO3 using PEI N-doped GQDs as reagent and stabilizer [18–24].

i) Citric acid (3.60 g, 18.75 mmol) and PEI (2.34 g, 1.80 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized distilled water incubated in an autoclave at 250 °C for 5 h. The PEI N-doped GQDs were obtained from the supernatant and were collected upon evaporation of the solution.

ii) A solution of PEI N-doped GQDs (1 g) in 100 mL water was added to a 250 mL round bottom flask. Then 20 mL of 0.1 M AgNO3 solution was added into the PEI N-doped GQDs mixture. The mixtures were heated to 90 °C for 2 h to complete the reduction of silver cation (Ag+) to silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) process to form nanocomposites consisting of silver nanoparticles and PEI N-doped GQDs (AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs) as the results. AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs solutions then were cooled to room temperature (RT) and stored at 4C for use (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Infrared absorption spectra were obtained from a Perkin Elmer BX II spectrometer in KBr discs and were reported in cm−1 units. PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs were morphologically characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Technai G2 STwin) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The nanocomposites in aqueous suspensions were treated in an ultrasonic bath before dropping to reduce aggregation. A 10 μL of distributed nanostructures dropped on formvar/carbon-coated 200 mesh copper grids and dried under ambient conditions. Elemental compositions of the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites materials were analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Bruker AXs XFlash Detector 4010).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs in H2O were recorded by Shimadzu UV-1800 scanning spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra of the studied nanocomposites were measured on Perkin Elmer LS55 spectrophotometer. Ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy measurements were performed in femtosecond timescale by using a mode-locked Ti: Sapphire laser amplifier and an optical parametric amplifier system with 52 fs pulse duration and 1 kHz repetition rate (Spectra Physics, Spitfire Pro XP, TOPAS). A commercial pump probe experimental setup (Spectra Physics, Helios) with a white light continuum probe was used to examine charge transfer dynamics of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs in H2O. The pump wavelength for ultrafast pump probe spectroscopy experiments was chosen based on the linear absorption spectra. The excited-state dynamics were measured between 0.1 ps and 3.2 ns timescale. The transient absorption data were collected by a fiber-coupled spectrometer connected to a computer. The femtosecond transient experimental data were analyzed by Surface Xplorer software that is supported by Ultrafast Systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cells (1000 cells/well) were inoculated into 96-well plates. Cells were incubated with materials at 100 µg / mL. After 4 h of incubation, cells were washed with DPBS, fixed with cold methanol, counterstained with DAPI. Images were taken by using an inverted fluorescent microscope.

A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cells (1000 cells/well) were inoculated into 96-well plates. Cells were incubated with materials at 100 µg / mL. After 4 h of incubation, cells were washed with DPBS. At 24 h, the LDH assay (Thermo) was performed according to the kit’s protocol. The percentage of cell viability was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs (FT-IR, TEM, EDX)
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and morphology of the PEI N doped GQDs and AgNPs / PEI N doped GQDs nanocomposites were also confirmed by their TEM images as shown in Fig. 1 . The transmission electron microscopic analysis confirms the presence of the PEI N doped GQDs and spherical shape morphology of the AgNPs/PEI N doped GQDs nanocomposites with particle sizes of around 9 to 17 nm and 6 to 13 nm (Fig. 1).

The presence of additional elements in the analyzed structures was also confirmed by the application of other methods of measurements, for example, Scanning Electron Microscopy with the EDX detector. The amount of elements detected by using EDX methods is given in Figs. S3 and S4 . EDX spectrum is shown in Fig. S3 ; carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are detected on the surface of samples. As seen from the EDX spectrum in Fig. S4 ; carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and silver are detected on the surface of the samples, and the presence of silver on the surface is a striking difference. Here, EDX is the fact that only the elements on the surface have detected the content. It never gives a complete elemental analysis. Nevertheless, the EDX data confirm the presence of silver in the samples.

Fig. 2 shows the steady-state absorption and emission spectra of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites in aqueous suspensions. The UV–Vis spectra of the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites shows two bands at 240 nm and 343 nm and 248 nm and 355 nm which are assigned to the π–π* and n–π* transition of C = C, and C-N and C-O, respectively (Fig. 2). In addition, the shoulder at 422 nm is assigned to the plasmon resonance for AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites (Fig. 2). Metal nanoparticles exhibit the absorption of visible electromagnetic waves by the collective release of conductive electrons on the surface. This surface is known as the plasmon resonance effect. The presence of metal nanoparticles can be determined from the nanocomposite spectrum using a UV–Visible spectrometer [47]. The plasmon absorption peak is specific for each metal. The exact position of the specific plasmon absorption peak depends on several factors such as particle size, shape, organization, and solvent [48].

The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs materials in an aqueous medium are shown in Fig. 2. The PEI N-doped GQDs show a strong emission spectrum at a maximum of 446 nm with 356 nm excitation wavelength. On the other hand, AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites indicate weak fluorescence intensity at a maximum of 446 nm with 344 nm excitation wavelength. Upon excitation of AgNPs / PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites at 344 nm, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity decreases as compared to PEI N-doped GQDs. The fluorescence quantum yields were calculated for the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs samples using Rodamine B as a reference compound in DCM solution. The fluorescence quantum yields were found at 0.091 and 0.034 for the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs, respectively. Moreover, the emission maximum wavelength remains the same whereas the emission intensity changes while varying the excitation wavelengths. This reveals that the emission maximum obtained at 446 nm for AgNPs / PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites is originated from PEI-N doped GQDs. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2, the fluorescence emission spectrum of PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites (λem = 446 nm) overlaps partially with their absorption spectra. Thus, it can be supposed that an energy transfer process takes place in the PEI N-doped GQDs and Ag metal NPs system, in which GQDs act as the donor and AgNP as the acceptors [49]. Therefore, one possible reason proposes for the obtained significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity is a decrease in the availability of hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxylic acid (COOH) groups in the surface of GQDs because some of them are oxidized to ketone (CO) and carbon dioxide (CO2) after reducing Ag+ to Ag0 [50]. This result demonstrates that AgNPs can be used as an “on–off” fluorescent sensor of PEI N-doped GQDs.

Inspired by the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we wonder whether there exists any electron/energy transfer between PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs and what kind of role the silver plays in these nanocomposite materials. To shed light on these mysteries, femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy experiments were carried out at 350 nm excitation.

To gain an insight into the electron/energy transfer dynamics, as well as decay kinetics of the studied GQDs, ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy measurements were performed. The pump wavelength was chosen as 350 nm for transient absorption measurements. In transient absorption spectra of PEI N-doped GQDs, there is a negative signal around 440 nm which is in the range of its fluorescence area (Fig. 3
a). Therefore, this negative signal is attributed to stimulated emission (SE). In addition, there is a broad positive signal which assigns to excited-state absorption (ESA) above 490 nm wavelength with a long lifetime. On the other hand, AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites demonstrate similar transient absorption features as compared to PEI N-doped GQDs as shown in Fig. 3 b. The spectra AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites show a negative signal which is compatible with its fluorescence range. Thus, this signal assigns to stimulated emission. Furthermore, there is a broad ESA signal localized above 480 nm wavelength decrease with time.

To determine excited state lifetime and the charge transfer rates, the decay traces of the studied compound were fitted by using a multiexponential fitting function. It is clearly seen from Fig. 4 that the lifetime of the excited state of PEI N-doped GQDs is greater than that of AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites due to the presence of AgNPs. This result proved that there is an electron transfer from PEI N-doped GQDs to AgNPs as seen in Fig. 4. According to the fitting results, the electron transfer rate is obtained as 1.5 ps. Notably, GQDs, a type of zero-dimension carbon nanomaterial that can be regarded as very tiny pieces of graphene possess higher peroxidase-like activity than graphene owing to their excellent electron transportation property. The –C–OH groups in GQDs are expected to be reduced during the preparation process of the AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites and the AgNPs on the surface of the GQDs can accelerate the electron transfer [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis, FMO, MEP surfaces and DOS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In the computational study, all GQDs were constructed from pristine graphene [52,53]. Since the nanocomposites that we have been working on experimentally are very large, the performance and efficiency of our computational system do not allow us to study them using the DFT method. Therefore, for similarity and simplicity, using the standard GQDs (-9-((4-carboxynaphthalen-2(1H)-ylidene)methyl)-5,8-dihydroxy-7-PEI-7H-isoindolo[6,7,1-def] quin oline-3-carboxylic acid, C55H79N15O6), As a model, we constructed N-doped GQDs containing hexagonal rings with H, OH, or/and COOH bonded to their edges, and PEI bonded to nitrogen in a quintuple ring for the PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites (Scheme. 2 ). It is well known that the UV–Vis spectra originated from the excitation of electrons play an important role in understanding the sensing mechanism of GQDs. In this context, as a contribution to the experimental data for the investigated nanocomposites, we have theoretically obtained the maximum UV–Vis absorption spectra by DFT with CAM-B3LYP basis set for the investigated nanocomposites. Although B3LYP is an effectively used method in computational quantum mechanical modeling to study the electronic structures of systems with many particles in the ground state, it has been shown that in the case of optical properties, the results of CAM-B3LYP functional are in better agreement with the experimental data [54–56]. Since the molecular geometry of the studied nanocomposites cannot be obtained from X-ray single crystal analysis due to some constraints, the possible geometries of these nanocomposites were built using the Gaussview 5.0 visualization program [57]. Subsequently, the UV–Vis absorption spectra were calculated in the Gaussian 09 W software package [58] using TD-DFT with the functional CAM-B3LYP- Lanl2dz basis set in water as solvent [59,60]. In addition, we have presented frontier molecular orbital (FMO) energies, molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces and the density of states (DOS) diagrams of the investigated nanocomposites by using Gauss–Sum v3.0 program [61]. The calculated and experimental maximum absorption wavelengths (λ max), the oscillator strength (f), major contributions, excitation and FMO energies are shown in Table 1 . According to the results in Table 1, the calculated maximum absorption wavelengths corresponding to the experimental data for PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite was observed at 348 and 251 nm, with excitation energies of 3.56 and 5.28 eV from H-6 to LUMO and H-6 to L + 3, HOMO to L + 7, H-17 to LUMO, H-10 to L + 3 electronic transitions with 75% and 36%, 17%, 15%, 10% major contributions, respectively. AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite shows maximum absorption wavelengths at 352 and 250 nm with excitation energies of 3.52 and 4.52 eV from HOMO to L + 6 and H-23 to LUMO, H-22 to L + 1, H-3 to L + 3 electronic transitions with 88% and 47%, 16%, 11% major contributions, respectively. It is well known that the absorption maxima frequently are related to excitations having larger oscillator strength [62]. In this context, we can say from Table 1 that the calculated λmax values for both nanocomposites are very close to the experimental λmax values.

The frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), called HOMOs (the highest occupied molecular orbitals) and LUMOs (the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals), are important in describing the ability of a molecule to absorb light. It has been presented HOMOs, LUMOs, MEP surfaces and DOS diagrams of the investigated nanocomposites in Fig. 5
(a) and (b). The DOS diagrams show that the energy gap between the HOMO (an electron donor region, green color) and LUMO (electron acceptor region, red color), which explains the charge transfer interaction, is calculated 3.10 and 1.78 eV in water for PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites, respectively. It can be said that the reason why the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite is smaller than that of the PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite is the interaction of the Ag atom with hydroxyl (–OH) group in the AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite. It can be concluded that the smaller HOMO-LUMO gap means that the valence electron in the HOMO can absorb low-energy light, causing the nanocomposites to appear blueish. At the same time, when we examine the DOS diagrams of the investigated nanocomposites, the presence of slightly more occupied energy levels in the virtual orbitals of the AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite compared to the PEI N-doped GQDs composite indicates that the sensing ability is better. The HOMO-LUMO visualization and MEP surfaces of both nanocomposites are also shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). Here the red and green colors of HOMO and LUMO visualization for both nanocomposites correspond to positive and negative orbital lobes, respectively. It can be seen that in the PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite, both HOMO and LUMO are not contributed by the polyethyleneimine group (only the N-doped GQDs part is contributed). In AgNPs / PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite, HOMO is contributed by the interaction Ag atom with the hydroxyl group, while LUMO is contributed by N-doped GQDs. The MEP surfaces in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), the red and yellow colors show the electron-rich region in terms of electrophilic reactivity (negative values) and the blue color shows the electron-poor region in terms of nucleophilic reactivity (positive values). It is interesting to note here that in the AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite, the electron-rich region is found only at the Ag atom, which is due to the interaction of the Ag atom with the hydroxyl group. The interaction of the Ag atom with the hydroxyl group is an electrophilic attraction, while the PEI N-doped GQDs exert a nucleophilic attraction.

A549, lung adenocarcinoma cells, were treated with PEI N-doped GQDs and Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs for 4 h and at the end of treatment, cells were visualized under the fluorescent microscope. According to Fig. 6
A, material-specific fluorescent signals were obtained from the cytoplasm of cells treated with Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs, whereas it was not obtained from N-doped GQDs. This data suggest that cells showed effective uptake of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs. When the viability of the cells were analyzed by LDH assay at 24 h, it was shown that both of the materials showed concentration dependent toxicity in A549 cells (Fig. 6B). The toxicity of PEI N-doped GQDs was higher compared to that of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs. This could be explained by the improved biocompatibility of these materials in response to the presence of AgNPs. Considering that it is even much more desirable to have therapeutic nanoparticles in addition to their imaging property, it can be concluded that AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs can be used in theranostic applications. Therefore, the fact that PEI N-doped GQDs and AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs reduce the viability of cancer cells suggests that they might have theranostic properties. Future studies can further study the toxicity of these materials in healthy tissues and evaluate their biodistribution profile in vivo. Furthermore, the materials can be engineered to improve the biocompatibility of these materials with improved targeting to cancer cells.

Metallic nanoparticles enhance the fluorescence properties of fluorophores in their proximity on account of the interaction between the surface plasmon of the metal nanoparticles and the dipole moment of fluorophores [63]. It has been determined that silver dots or silver particles deposited on a surface act as a scaffold in bioassays using the metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) technique. The glass surface covered with a film of silver dots increases the signal up to forty times [64]. As the fluorescence of the fluorophores increases due to the silver dots, the sensitivity of the fluorescent signals of the cell membrane may also increase. In addition, since the particle sizes of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites are smaller than PEI N-doped GQDs, the level of cellular uptake of Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs can be higher compared to the nanocomposite without Ag.

The first time, polyethyleneimine functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEI N-doped GQDs) containing N atoms were synthesized using the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and polyethyleneimine. Then, green synthesis of PEI N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) using PEI N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as reducing reagent and stabilizer. The synthesized AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites were confirmed by TEM, EDX, and FT-IR analyses. Since the absorption spectra of studied GQDs overlap partially with their emission spectra, it was assumed that there is an energy transfer between AgNPs and PEI N-doped GQDs. Performed femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy measurements based upon fluorescence overlapping and quenching mechanisms proved that there is an ultrafast electron/energy transfer from PEI N-doped GQDs to AgNPs.

According to the theoretical results, UV–Vis results of both nanocomposites exhibit a good agreement with experimental results concerning the dominant transitions. In addition, due to the smaller HOMO-LUMO energy gap, AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite shows low stability, high chemical reactivity and greater energy gain. It is also concluded that the sensing ability of AgNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposite is better than that of PEI N-doped GQDs.

When lung adenocarcinoma cells (A549) were treated with GQDs, material-specific fluorescent signals were obtained from the cytoplasm of cells only treated with Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs, as compared with N-doped GQDs. This indicates that cells readily uptake Ag/PEI N-doped GQDs.

Consequently, these findings encourage further investigation of the AgNPS/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites for potential optical, biological and medical applications. Future studies can delineate their potential by using in vivo bioimaging experimental models.
All data that support the findings of this study are included within the article (and any supplementary files).

Ahmet Karatay: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Diğdem Erdener: Resources, Data curation, Investigation. Cansu Gürcan: Investigation, Data curation. Elif Akhuseyin Yildiz: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Açelya Yilmazer: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Bahadır Boyacıoğlu: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Hüseyin Unver: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Mustafa Yıldız: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ayhan Elmali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113741.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
